attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department at 34 weeks gestation with a two-day history of headache and visual disturbances. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 178/105 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her reflexes are brisk with sustained ankle clonus.

Blood tests reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 122 g/L (115-160)
  • Platelet count 85 × 10⁹/L (150-400)
  • International Normalised Ratio 1.0 (0.8-1.2)
  • Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time 30 seconds (25-35)
  • Fibrinogen 4.0 g/L (2.0-4.0)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most likely to be abnormal in this case?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of pre-eclampsia with severe features, including thrombocytopenia, neurological symptoms (headache and visual disturbances), and severe hypertension.

Urine protein:creatinine ratio is most likely to be abnormal in this case. Pre-eclampsia is characterised by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks gestation with proteinuria or evidence of end-organ dysfunction. The urine protein:creatinine ratio is the preferred method for quantifying proteinuria in pre-eclampsia. A ratio >30 mg/mmol is diagnostic of significant proteinuria and would be expected to be elevated in this patient with severe pre-eclampsia.

D-dimer levels are elevated in normal pregnancy and are not specific for pre-eclampsia. While D-dimer would be elevated in disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), the normal fibrinogen and coagulation profile make DIC unlikely despite the thrombocytopenia.

ADAMTS13 activity testing is used in the diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP). While TTP can occur in pregnancy and may present with thrombocytopenia and neurological symptoms, the presence of severe hypertension is more consistent with pre-eclampsia, and ADAMTS13 activity would be expected to be normal.

Complement gene function testing is used to diagnose atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome (aHUS). While aHUS can occur in pregnancy and cause thrombocytopenia, the clinical picture is more consistent with pre-eclampsia given the severe hypertension, and complement gene function would be expected to be normal.

Anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies are used to diagnose systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). While SLE can occur in pregnancy and may present with neurological symptoms and thrombocytopenia, the acute presentation with severe hypertension is more consistent with pre-eclampsia, and these antibodies would be expected to be negative.
learningPoints: Pre-eclampsia is characterised by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks gestation with proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction.
Quantification of proteinuria is important for the diagnosis of pre-eclampsia.
references: Dimitriadis E, Rolnik DL, Zhou W, et al. Pre-eclampsia [published correction appears in Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2023 Jul 3;9(1):35. doi: 10.1038/s41572-023-00451-4]. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2023;9(1):8. Published 2023 Feb 16. doi:10.1038/s41572-023-00417-6
Cines DB, Levine LD. Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy. Blood. 2017;130(21):2271-2277. doi:10.1182/blood-2017-05-781971

Lowe SA, Bowyer L, Lust K, McMahon LP, Morton M, North RA, Paech M, Said JM. SOMANZ guidelines for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy 2014. Aust N Z J Obstet Gynaecol. 2015 Oct;55(5):e1-29. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26412014/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
